title: Model Checkpoints
lesson:
A model checkpoint is a saved snapshot of a model's state at one moment in time. Think of it like a save file in a video game. If the boss kills you, you do not start the whole game over — you reload the last save and try again. A checkpoint does the same job for training: it lets you stop, reload, and continue without throwing away hours (or weeks) of expensive work.

What is actually inside a checkpoint?

The most important thing in a checkpoint is the weights — the millions or billions of numbers the model has learned (see Parameters). But a training checkpoint usually holds more than just weights. It also saves the optimizer state: the optimizer is the part of training that decides how much to nudge each weight, and it keeps running notes (like the average direction of recent changes). It often saves the step count, the learning-rate schedule (how fast the model is currently learning), and the random-number seed.

Why save all that extra baggage? Imagine you are following a long recipe and have to stop halfway. Saving only the half-cooked dish is not enough — you also need to know which step you were on, the oven temperature, and how much salt you have already added. Reload only the weights and you can use the model, but resuming training would lurch because the optimizer forgot its momentum. So checkpoints come in two flavours: a heavy resume-training checkpoint with everything, and a lighter inference-only checkpoint with just the weights for running the model (see Training vs Inference).

Why checkpoints matter in real life

Training a large model can run for days on rented GPUs that cost real money. Machines crash. Cloud instances get reclaimed. If you saved nothing, a crash at hour 40 means starting from hour 0. With checkpoints written every, say, 500 steps, you lose only minutes of work. Checkpoints also let you compare: you might save one every epoch (one full pass over your data) and discover that the checkpoint from epoch 2 is actually better than epoch 5, because the model started overfitting — memorising the training data instead of learning general patterns. You simply keep the good save and discard the rest.

What a checkpoint looks like on disk

In the Hugging Face world, saving during a fine-tune produces a folder, not a single file:

checkpoint-1500/
  model.safetensors        # the weights
  optimizer.pt             # optimizer state (only for resuming)
  scheduler.pt             # learning-rate schedule
  trainer_state.json       # step count, loss history
  config.json              # model shape/settings

The weights live in a .safetensors file. Safetensors is the modern, safe format: older .bin / .pt files used Python pickle, which can hide malicious code that runs the moment you load it. Safetensors stores only plain numbers, so loading a downloaded checkpoint can't execute anything nasty. Always prefer it.

Saving and loading in practice

from transformers import TrainingArguments

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./out",
    save_steps=500,            # write a checkpoint every 500 steps
    save_total_limit=3,        # keep only the 3 newest, delete older ones
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
)
# Resume a crashed run from the last save:
trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=True)

Two settings here save real grief. save_total_limit stops your disk filling up — each checkpoint of a big model can be tens of gigabytes, and they pile up fast. load_best_model_at_end automatically keeps the checkpoint with the best validation score, not just the last one.

Checkpoints vs final models vs adapters

People mix these up. A checkpoint is an intermediate save during training. A final model is the polished version you ship. And if you used LoRA or QLoRA, your checkpoint might be tiny — only the small adapter weights, not the whole base model. That is why a LoRA checkpoint can be a few megabytes while a full-model checkpoint is many gigabytes.

Resume checkpointInference checkpoint
Weights + optimizer + scheduler + step countJust the weights (+ config)
Large; for continuing trainingSmaller; for running the model
Saved mid-run, often deleted laterThe version you actually deploy

💡 Rule of thumb: save often enough that a crash costs minutes not hours, but not so often that writing to disk slows training. Every few hundred steps, keeping the best 2–3, is a sane default.
mentalModel: A checkpoint is a save file in a video game. Saving only the weights is like saving your character but forgetting which level you were on — fine for showing off the character, useless for continuing the quest. A full training checkpoint remembers the level, the score, and your position, so you can pick up exactly where you left off.
mistakes: Saving only the weights, then trying to resume training. Without the optimizer and scheduler state, resuming makes the model lurch because the optimizer lost its momentum and the learning rate restarts wrong. Save the full checkpoint when you intend to continue.
Letting checkpoints fill the disk. Each save of a large model can be tens of gigabytes. Without save_total_limit, a long run silently fills the drive and crashes hours in. Cap how many you keep.

Assuming the last checkpoint is the best. Models often overfit late in training — they memorise the training set and get worse on new data. Track a validation score and keep the best checkpoint, not automatically the final one.
Trusting .bin / pickle files from strangers. The old pickle format can run hidden code on load. Prefer .safetensors, which stores only numbers and cannot execute anything.
Confusing a LoRA adapter checkpoint with a full model. A tiny adapter checkpoint is useless on its own — you still need the matching base model to run it. Save or note which base it belongs to.

Forgetting to save the tokenizer and config alongside the weights. Reload the weights with a mismatched tokenizer or config and the model produces garbage. Keep them in the same folder so the checkpoint is self-contained.
exercise:
Start a tiny fine-tune (even on a small model and a few dozen examples) with the Hugging Face Trainer. Set save_steps=50 and save_total_limit=2. Let it run past 150 steps, then stop it by hand. Look in output_dir and confirm you see exactly two checkpoint-* folders, and that the older ones were deleted. Open one folder and list the files inside it.

Stretch goal: Restart the script with trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=True) and watch the logs. Confirm the step counter picks up where it stopped (e.g. step 150) rather than restarting at 0 — proving the optimizer and scheduler state were restored, not just the weights.
solution: Hint: Inside a checkpoint-100/ folder you should find model.safetensors (weights), optimizer.pt and scheduler.pt (the resume state), and trainer_state.json (step count and loss history). Because save_total_limit=2, only the two newest folders survive — older ones are auto-deleted. On resume, the log line will read something like Continuing training from step 150; if it restarted at 0, the checkpoint wasn't found or only the weights were loaded. That step-resume working is the proof that a checkpoint is more than just weights.
summary: A model checkpoint is a saved snapshot of training — like a video-game save file. A resume checkpoint holds weights plus optimizer and scheduler state so you can continue exactly where you stopped; an inference checkpoint holds just the weights for running the model. Save often (every few hundred steps), cap how many you keep, prefer the safe .safetensors format, and keep the best validation checkpoint rather than blindly the last one.

keyTerms.def: A saved snapshot of a model's state during training, so you can stop, reload, and continue without losing work.
The learned numbers that make up the model. They are the core of any checkpoint.
The optimizer's running notes (like momentum) about how to nudge each weight. Needed to resume training smoothly, not just to run the model.
The plan for how fast the model learns over time. Saved so a resumed run continues at the right learning speed.
A safe file format that stores only plain numbers, so loading a checkpoint can't run hidden code — unlike the older pickle-based .bin/.pt files.
One full pass over the entire training dataset. People often save a checkpoint at the end of each epoch.
When a model memorises the training data instead of learning general patterns, so it gets worse on new inputs. A reason to keep an earlier checkpoint.
A full save (weights + optimizer + scheduler + step count) used to continue an interrupted training run.
A lighter save with just the weights and config, used to run the finished model rather than keep training it.
